title: How to Use a Wooden Spurtle to De‑glaze Cast Iron Skillets After Baking
meta_description: Deglaze cast iron skillets quickly with a wooden spurtle. Learn techniques and why our 2 PCS set is ideal for bakers.
html:
Cast iron skillets are baking and cooking workhorses, but their nonstick surfaces demand care after use. Deglaze cast iron with a wooden spurtle to remove stubborn food bits and residues cleanly—no harsh scrubbing or abrasive pads. A wooden spurtle, like the 2 PCS Wooden Spurtle & Sourdough Starter Stirrer Set, is the perfect tool for this task. Its slender, durable design scrapes without scratching, and it doubles as a sourdough starter stirrer. Let’s break down how to use it effectively.

Why a Wooden Spurtle Excels at Deglazing

Cast iron retains heat and develops a natural nonstick layer over time, but this also means food often sticks after baking. Metal tools can scratch the seasoning, while plastic ones lack durability. The wooden spurtle’s firm, smooth surface lifts debris with minimal force. The 2 PCS set includes a spurtle spatula and a Danish dough stick—perfect for scraping jars and mixing before deglazing.

Step-by-Step: Deglaze Cast Iron with a Wooden Spurtle

  1. After baking, let the skillet cool slightly (avoid sudden temperature changes).
  2. Add 1-2 cups of water, broth, or wine directly into the pan. The liquid helps loosen stuck-on bits.
  3. Use the wooden spurtle’s edge to gently scrape the bottom and sides. Work in circular motions to dislodge debris.
  4. Combine the liquid and loosened food, then deglaze with your chosen liquid (wine, vinegar, or stock). Simmer to create a sauce or reduce for flavor.
  5. Rinse the skillet, dry thoroughly, and apply a thin layer of oil to maintain seasoning.

Tips for Using Your Wooden Spurtle

Maximize your tool’s versatility by storing it in a dry place and oiling it occasionally with food-safe mineral oil. The skinny design of the 2 PCS set makes it ideal for reaching into corners and narrow jars. For sourdough starters, use the same spurtle to mix and feed cultures—no need for extra tools.

Common Mistakes to Avoid

Don’t submerge your cast iron in water for long periods. Focus on deglazing with the wooden spurtle to preserve seasoning. Also, avoid using the spurtle on glass or ceramic surfaces—the tool is optimized for cast iron, stainless steel, or mixing jars.

FAQ

Can I use the wooden spurtle on other cookware?

Absolutely. Its non-scratch surface works on stainless steel, enameled pots, and mixing jars—just avoid glass or plastic, where it might slip.

How do I clean the wooden spurtle?

Rinse it under warm water after use. Hand dry immediately and oil occasionally to prevent cracking.

Is the 2 PCS set enough for heavy use?

Yes. The set includes two tools for dual tasks (scrapping and mixing). Replace when you notice wear or warping.
